outline.heading: SDS-PAGE analysis
outline.preview: # SDS-PAGE analysis 20 $\mu$ l of crude supernatant were analyzed by electrophoresis on a 10% NuPAGE Bis-Tris-acetate sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel (SDS-PAGE) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Villebon-sur-Yvette, France). After migration, the gel was subject to colloidal blue staining.

outline.heading: Primary enzymatic activity detection on yeast transformants
outline.preview: # Primary enzymatic activity detection on yeast transformants Cells were grown for 24 h and then removed via centrifugation (6000 rpm for 20 min, 28 °C). Xylanase, cellulase, or protease activity levels in the crude supernatants were monitored using adapted fluorescence assays in a Spectramax M5 spectrofluorometer (Molecular Devices, Wokingham, UK).

outline.heading: Xylanase assay
outline.preview: # Xylanase assay 25 $\mu$ l of crude supernatant was added to 25 $\mu$ l of a 300 $\mu$ M solution of $\beta$ -D-xylobioside-6,8-difluoro-7-hydroxycoumarin-4-methansulfonate in acetate buffer (100 mM, pH 4.5). The reaction was monitored at 25 ${}^{\circ}$ C for 30 min ( $\lambda_{ex} = 360$ nm, $\lambda_{em} = 460$ nm).

outline.heading: Cellobiohydrolase assay
outline.preview: # Cellobiohydrolase assay 25 $\mu$ l of crude supernatant was added to 25 $\mu$ l of a 300 $\mu$ M solution of $\beta$ -D-cellobioside-6,8-difluoro-7-hydroxycoumarin-4-methansulfonate in acetate buffer (100 mM, pH 4.5). The reaction was monitored at 25 ${}^{\circ}$ C for 30 min ( $\lambda_{ex} = 360$ nm, $\lambda_{em} = 460$ nm).

outline.heading: Protease assay
outline.preview: # Protease assay 25 $\mu$ l of crude supernatant was added to 25 $\mu$ l of a 50 $\mu$ g/ml solution of Enzchek Protease Assay substrate (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Villebon-sur-Yvette, France) in acetate buffer (100 mM, pH 4.0). The reaction was monitored at 25 ${}^{\circ}$ C for 30 min ( $\lambda_{ex} = 480$ nm, $\lambda_{em} = 518$ nm).

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems is a major bottleneck for creating biocatalysts via directed evolution. Droplet-based microfluidics offers higher throughput and lower volumes than microtiter plates, but most prior systems used cytoplasmic expression in E. coli, requiring lysis or membrane transport. The yeast Yarrowia lipolytica has excellent secretion abilities, making it ideal for extracellular enzyme expression in droplets, but it had never been used in droplet microfluidics. The goal was to develop a droplet-based microfluidic HTS platform using Y. lipolytica for secreted enzymes, enabling efficient screening of large libraries.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Droplet-based microfluidics is an attractive HTS alternative, but most enzymes are expressed in E. coli cytoplasm, requiring lysis. Y. lipolytica's secretion ability is exploited to build an extracellular expression system. Five A. niger hydrolytic genes were expressed and secreted actively. A droplet microfluidic platform was developed for single-cell encapsulation, growth, substrate injection, incubation, detection, and sorting. Xylanase, cellobiohydrolase, and protease activities were detected with low variability. The system was used to screen for thermostable xylanase C variants, achieving up to 4.7-fold improvement. The platform enables HTS of recombinant enzymes at 10^5 strains/h.
process_signals: Problem: need for HTS of secreted enzymes without lysis
Solution: use Y. lipolytica secretion in droplets
Achievement: successful detection and sorting of active variants
methods: Droplet microfluidics for encapsulation, growth, assay, sorting

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: HTS is a bottleneck in directed evolution. Microtiter plates are costly and low-throughput. Droplet microfluidics offers picoliter volumes and kHz manipulation. Most systems use E. coli cytoplasmic expression, requiring lysis or membrane transport. Yeasts can secrete eukaryotic proteins. Y. lipolytica is a model for secretion, with genetic tools and high yields. Developing a droplet HTS with Y. lipolytica would advance microbial cell factory development. Cellulases and xylanases are important for biomass conversion; proteases are major industrial enzymes. Improving their properties (e.g., thermostability) is valuable.
process_signals: Motivation: overcome HTS bottleneck
Rationale: Y. lipolytica secretion avoids lysis
Goal: develop droplet HTS for Y. lipolytica secreted enzymes

section_gists.heading: Results and discussion
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Expression of fungal genes in Yarrowia lipolytica
section_gists.gist: Five A. niger genes (xlnb, xlnc, cbha, egla, pep1) were codon-optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566 with a zeta docking platform for consistent integration. All five enzymes were successfully overproduced and secreted as major proteins in crude supernatant without extensive optimization.
process_signals: Design: codon optimization, zeta platform for fair comparison
Success: all five enzymes secreted at high levels

section_gists.heading: Enzymatic activity of the secreted proteins
section_gists.gist: Activity was assayed in crude supernatants using fluorogenic substrates. Xylanase activity was detected for xlnb and xlnc; cellobiohydrolase activity for cbha; low endocellulase activity for egla on cellobiose substrate; protease activity for pep1. All enzymes were active after secretion. egla showed low activity, likely due to substrate specificity.
process_signals: Validation: all enzymes active
Partial failure: egla low activity on cellobiose substrate (endocellulase vs. cellobiohydrolase)
failures_or_limits: egla (endoglucanase A) showed low activity on cellobiose substrate, likely due to substrate specificity

section_gists.heading: Droplet-based microfluidic HTS platform
section_gists.gist: The platform consists of a dropmaker for single-cell encapsulation in 20-pl droplets and an integrated screening device for picoinjection of substrate, on-chip incubation, fluorescence detection, and sorting.
process_signals: Design: two-device system for encapsulation and screening
methods: Integrated device: picoinjection, incubation, detection, sorting

section_gists.heading: Encapsulation and growth of Y. lipolytica in 20 pl droplets
section_gists.gist: Y. lipolytica had not been used in droplets before. Challenges: cell adhesion to hydrophobic channels, aggregation, sedimentation. Solutions: added 0.1% TWEEN 20, used small inner diameter tubing (100 µm) for shear, continuous stirring. Achieved single-cell encapsulation with Poisson distribution (λ=0.03–0.1). Droplets produced at 2500–3000/s. Cells grew in droplets after 16 h incubation.
process_signals: Problem: Y. lipolytica adhesion and aggregation in microfluidic channels
Revision: added detergent, small tubing, stirring
Success: reliable single-cell encapsulation and growth
failures_or_limits: Initial adhesion and aggregation without optimization

section_gists.heading: Detection of enzymatic activities in droplets
section_gists.gist: Xylanase strains were encoded with sulforhodamine B (10 and 50 µM) and co-encapsulated. After growth, substrate was picoinjected. Both strains showed clear positive populations with low variability (CV ~3–7%). Cellobiohydrolase and protease activities were also detected in ternary emulsion, but with lower signal and higher variability. Protease activity was low, possibly due to substrate accessibility or enzyme properties.
process_signals: Success: xylanase detection with low variability
Partial failure: cellobiohydrolase and protease signals lower, more variable
Encoding: optical barcoding with sulforhodamine B
failures_or_limits: Cellobiohydrolase and protease activities were lower and more variable than xylanase

section_gists.heading: Screening for thermostable mutants of the endo-β-1,4-xylanase C
section_gists.gist: Xylanase C was less thermostable than B (3.7% vs 71.7% residual activity after 90°C, 30 s). Error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc were created. Heat shock conditions were optimized: 90°C for 30 s in droplets. Sorting gates were set to collect top 0.1% of droplets. Sorted clones were retested in microtiter plates; several showed improved thermostability (up to 4.7-fold). Sequencing revealed mutations. The platform successfully enriched thermostable variants.
process_signals: Hypothesis: droplet HTS can identify thermostable xylanase C variants
Design: error-prone PCR library, heat shock in droplets
Optimization: heat shock conditions (90°C, 30 s)
Validation: microtiter plate retest, sequencing
Success: up to 4.7-fold improvement
failures_or_limits: Need to optimize heat shock conditions for droplet format

section_gists.heading: Conclusion
section_gists.gist: The droplet microfluidic HTS with Y. lipolytica enables rapid, efficient kinetic analysis of secreted enzymes at single-cell level. The technique can be extended to any secreted enzyme with a fluorescent assay. It provides a precise, high-throughput, low-cost screening method for directed evolution.
process_signals: Achievement: platform validated for HTS of secreted enzymes
Limitation: only for secreted enzymes with fluorescent assays
failures_or_limits: Limited to secreted enzymes and compatible fluorescent assays

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Y. lipolytica secretion can be combined with droplet microfluidics to create a HTS platform for extracellular enzymes, avoiding lysis.
The platform can detect multiple enzyme activities with low variability and be used for directed evolution (e.g., thermostability).
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why was cellobiohydrolase and protease activity lower/more variable in droplets?
Can the platform be extended to non-hydrolytic enzymes or intracellular enzymes?
What are the specific mutations in the improved xylanase C variants?

events.summary: Lack of rapid and generalized HTS systems is a major bottleneck for creating biocatalysts via directed evolution.
Most droplet-based microfluidic systems use cytoplasmic expression in E. coli, requiring lysis or membrane transport for enzyme assay.
Y. lipolytica's secretion abilities can be exploited to build an extracellular expression system for droplet-based microfluidic HTS.
Five A. niger hydrolytic genes were codon-optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566 with a zeta docking platform for consistent integration.
All five enzymes were successfully overproduced and secreted as major proteins in crude supernatant without extensive optimization.
All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion, as assayed with fluorogenic substrates.
Endoglucanase A (egla) displayed low activity on cellobiose substrate, likely due to substrate specificity.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems for secreted enzymes in directed evolution, due to reliance on cytoplasmic expression in E. coli requiring lysis.

canonical_events.summary: Lack of rapid and generalized HTS systems is a major bottleneck for directed evolution of biocatalysts.
Most droplet-based microfluidic systems use cytoplasmic expression in E. coli, requiring lysis or membrane transport for enzyme assay.
Y. lipolytica's secretion abilities can be exploited to build an extracellular expression system for droplet-based microfluidic HTS, avoiding lysis.
Five A. niger hydrolytic genes (xlnb, xlnc, cbha, egla, pep1) were codon-optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566 with a zeta docking platform for consistent integration.
All five enzymes were successfully overproduced and secreted as major proteins in crude supernatant without extensive optimization.
All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion, as assayed with fluorogenic substrates.
Endoglucanase A (egla) displayed low activity on cellobiose substrate, likely due to substrate specificity (endocellulase vs. cellobiohydrolase).

Encapsulation of single Y. lipolytica cells in 20 pl droplets was optimized to prevent adhesion and aggregation using 0.1% TWEEN 20, 100 µm tubing, and continuous stirring; droplets produced at 2500–3000/s with Poisson loading (λ=0.03–0.1).
Y. lipolytica cells grew and secreted enzymes in droplets after 16 h incubation.

Xylanase activity of two strains (xlnb and xlnc) was detected in droplets with low variability (CV 3.1% and 3%) after picoinjection of substrate and on-chip incubation, using optical encoding with sulforhodamine B.
Low variability in monoclonal populations is attributed to the Y. lipolytica expression system and precise control of incubation time via delay line design.
Cellobiohydrolase and protease activities were detected in droplets, but with lower signal and higher variability compared to xylanase.
Error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc gene were generated with low (library A, ~2 mutations/kb) and medium (library B, >4 mutations/kb) mutation frequencies.
Both libraries were analyzed using the droplet-based microfluidic HTS system before and after a heat shock of 90 °C for 30 s.

Before heat shock, 27% of clones in library A and 9% in library B displayed xylanase activity; none had greater activity than wild-type. After heat shock, 1% (8 clones) in library A and 0.17% (1 clone) in library B had higher activities than wild-type.
key_numbers: 27% (216 clones) active in library A before heat shock
9% (54 clones) active in library B before heat shock
1% (8 clones) improved in library A after heat shock
0.17% (1 clone) improved in library B after heat shock

canonical_events.summary: Microtiter plate screening of 480 clones from library A identified six thermostable mutants (1.25%), similar to droplet-based HTS results; sequencing revealed 1–6 point mutations (average 2.5) with higher density at N-terminus.
The platform is limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays; cellobiohydrolase and protease detection showed lower signal and higher variability.

Gene design enables successful overexpression and secretion.
Secretion allows validation of enzyme activity.
While most enzymes are active, egla shows low activity, revealing a limitation.

Optimized encapsulation allows cell growth and secretion in droplets.
Growth and secretion enable xylanase activity detection with low variability.
Low variability is analyzed and attributed to expression system and delay line.
After xylanase, cellobiohydrolase and protease activities are tested, showing lower performance.
Lower signal and variability for some enzymes contribute to platform limitation.
Successful xylanase detection motivates library creation for thermostability screening.
Libraries are used in droplet HTS with heat shock.
Heat shock experiment yields activity data before and after treatment.
Microtiter plate retest and sequencing validate droplet HTS results and identify mutations.
Validation leads to overall platform limitation statement.

findings.problem: Step 5 claims encapsulation parameters (0.1% TWEEN 20, 100 µm tubing, λ=0.03–0.1) that are not directly supported by the provided evidence. The evidence only mentions stirring and droplet production rate, not the specific surfactant concentration, tubing size, or Poisson loading range.
findings.revision_instruction: Either provide evidence for these parameters or remove them from the step description. Ensure all claimed numbers are traceable to evidence.

findings.problem: Step 6 reports specific positive droplet percentages (2.7% and 2.4%) and CV values (3.1% and 3%) that are not directly quoted in the provided evidence. The evidence only mentions 'CV of 3%' and a partial quote for 2.7%.
findings.revision_instruction: Include evidence that explicitly states the 2.4% positive droplet percentage and the 3.1% CV, or adjust the step to reflect only the supported numbers.

findings.problem: Step 11 mentions a hit rate of 1.25% (6/480) which is not directly quoted in the evidence. The evidence states 480 clones were isolated and six thermostable mutants were identified, but the percentage is calculated.
findings.revision_instruction: Either provide a quote that states the 1.25% hit rate or present the raw numbers without the calculated percentage.

findings.problem: Step 8 claims cellobiohydrolase and protease detection showed 'lower signal and higher variability compared to xylanase', but the provided evidence (EV_C002_E05_01, EV_C002_E05_02) states 'comparable narrow signal distribution' and that the system is 'efficient and reliable'. The step's observation may overstate the limitation.
findings.revision_instruction: Align the observation with the evidence: note that activity was detected with comparable narrow signal distribution, but acknowledge any relative differences if supported by other evidence.

findings.problem: Step 12 states the platform is limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays, but the evidence for cellobiohydrolase and protease actually demonstrates successful detection. The limitation is more about substrate specificity (egla) and potential signal differences, not a strict limitation to secreted enzymes.
findings.revision_instruction: Refine the limitation statement to reflect that the platform works for multiple secreted enzymes but may require optimization for some substrates, rather than implying it is limited only to secreted enzymes.

self_critic.summary: The candidate covers all required mainline events and generally follows the correct order. However, several steps include specific numbers (encapsulation parameters, positive droplet percentages, CV values, hit rate) that are not directly supported by the provided evidence. Additionally, the description of cellobiohydrolase/protease detection and the final limitation statement slightly misrepresent the evidence. Revisions should ensure all claimed numbers are traceable to evidence and that observations align with the quoted results.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lack of rapid and generalized HTS systems is a major bottleneck for directed evolution of biocatalysts. Most droplet-based microfluidic systems use cytoplasmic expression in E. coli, requiring lysis.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for an extracellular expression system to avoid lysis step in droplet-based microfluidic HTS.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Y. lipolytica's secretion abilities can be exploited to build an extracellular expression system for droplet-based microfluidic HTS, avoiding lysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Y. lipolytica as host for secreted enzyme expression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Y. lipolytica is known for excellent secretion abilities.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design expression constructs for target enzymes.

evidence.quote_or_span: The lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems has proven to be a major bottleneck when it comes to creating biocatalysts via directed evolution and protein engineering approaches.
most of the enzymes screened to date are expressed within the cytoplasm of Escherichia coli cells, which means that a lysis step must take place inside the droplets for enzyme activity to be assayed.
we take advantage of the excellent secretion abilities of the yeast Yarrowia lipolytica to describe a highly efficient expression system particularly suitable for the droplet-based microfluidic HTS.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis to use Y. lipolytica secretion for HTS.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to express and secrete target enzymes in Y. lipolytica.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Codon-optimized A. niger genes integrated at zeta docking platform will yield high secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design expression cassettes for five A. niger hydrolytic genes (xlnb, xlnc, cbha, egla, pep1) in Y. lipolytica JMY2566 with zeta docking platform.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Five genes were codon-optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566 with a zeta docking platform for consistent integration.

evidence.quote_or_span: The five genes were codon optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566
This strain possesses a zeta docking platform that favors integration at that specific locus

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the enzymes be overproduced and secreted?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Enzymes will be secreted as major proteins in supernatant.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Grow transformants and analyze crude supernatant by SDS-PAGE.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All five enzymes were successfully overproduced and secreted as major proteins in crude supernatant without extensive optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each of the target enzymes was successfully overproduced and secreted as a major protein in the crude supernatant.
All the enzymes demonstrated high expression levels without requiring any extensive optimization of growth conditions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the secreted enzymes active?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secreted enzymes retain activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assay activity with fluorogenic substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion. Endoglucanase A (egla) displayed low activity on cellobiose substrate, likely due to substrate specificity.

evidence.quote_or_span: All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can Y. lipolytica cells be encapsulated and grown in droplets?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Optimized encapsulation conditions will allow single-cell growth and secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate single Y. lipolytica cells in 20 pl droplets with continuous stirring; produce droplets at 2500–3000/s.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Droplets produced at 2500–3000/s; cells grew and secreted enzymes after 16 h incubation.

evidence.quote_or_span: cell suspensions were also continuously stirred during the encapsulation process to prevent cell sedimentation
Droplets were produced at a rate of 2500 to 3000 per second via hydrodynamic flow focusing

the droplets contained dozens of cells after incubation, showing that Y. lipolytica was able to replicate in the droplets.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can xylanase activity be detected with low variability?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Xylanase activity will show low CV due to uniform expression and controlled incubation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Picoinject xylobiose-based coumarin substrate, incubate on-chip, and measure fluorescence; use sulforhodamine B for optical encoding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Xylanase activity of two strains (xlnb and xlnc) was detected with low variability (CV 3%). Positive droplet percentage for xlnb was 2.7%.

evidence.quote_or_span: Both strains showed remarkably low variability in their signal distributions.
coefficient of variation [CV] of 3%

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why is variability low?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low variability is due to Y. lipolytica expression system and delay line design.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attribute low variability to constitutive promoter and uniform incubation time.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low variability attributed to Y. lipolytica expression system and precise control of incubation time via delay line design.

evidence.quote_or_span: This narrow phenotypic distribution at the single cell level within a monoclonal population is highly desirable for enzymatic screening applications and is due to a combination of two factors.
the expression system developed in Y. lipolytica under a strong constitutive promoter is characterized by low variability in expression levels
the delay line structure design, made of constrictions and expansions, ensured that average droplet residence time, and hence incubation time, displayed little variance

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can cellobiohydrolase and protease be detected?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cellobiohydrolase and protease will show activity with comparable narrow signal distribution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assay cellobiohydrolase (cbha) and protease (pep1) with respective fluorogenic substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cellobiohydrolase and protease activities were detected with comparable narrow signal distribution, demonstrating efficient and reliable detection.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each strain showed hydrolytic activity against the respective substrate with comparable narrow signal distribution, while no activity was detected for the other strains.
These results demonstrate that our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system is an efficient and reliable method for detecting endoxylanase, cellobiohydrolase, and protease activity.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create mutant libraries for thermostability screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc will yield variants with altered thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc gene with low (library A, ~2 mutations/kb) and medium (library B, >4 mutations/kb) mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries A and B generated.

evidence.quote_or_span: Libraries were generated from xlnc gene using error-prone PCR.
We built libraries of error-prone xlnc variants using a two-step PCR amplification according to [30].

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can thermostable variants be enriched by heat shock?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Heat shock at 90 °C for 30 s will inactivate less stable variants, enriching thermostable ones.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate libraries, apply heat shock (90 °C, 30 s), and assay xylanase activity before and after.

tool_or_method.name: Integrated microfluidic screening device with heat shock module

evolution_trajectory.observation: Before heat shock, 27% of clones in library A and 9% in library B displayed xylanase activity; none had greater activity than wild-type. After heat shock, 1% (8 clones) in library A and 0.17% (1 clone) in library B had higher activities than wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate hits by microtiter plate and sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the droplet-based microfluidic HTS system described above, both libraries were analyzed before and after a heat shock of 90 °C for 30 s
The JMY4510 (xlnc) wild-type, library A, and library B were encapsulated in 20 pl droplets as described above.
To generate the heat shock, the droplets were run through PTFE tubing (0.56 mm × 1.07 mm) immersed in a 90 °C water bath. The flow rate was set such that residence time within the tubing was 30 s.
The droplets were then collected in a glass vial and reloaded into the integrated screening device to assay post-heat-shock levels of xylanase activity.

After exposure to heat shock, 1% of the clones (8 clones) in library A were found to have higher activities than the wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ]), while this percentage was 0.17% (1 clone) for library B.

Before exposure to heat shock, 27% of the clones (216 clones) in library A and 9% of the clones (54 clones) in library B displayed xylanase activity. None of these clones were found to have greater activity than wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ).

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Microtiter plate retest will confirm thermostability; sequencing will reveal mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Isolate 480 clones from library A, retest in microtiter plates, and sequence six confirmed thermostable mutants.

tool_or_method.name: Microtiter plate assay and Sanger sequencing

evolution_trajectory.observation: Microtiter plate screening identified six thermostable mutants, similar to droplet-based HTS results. Sequencing revealed 1–6 point mutations (average 2.5) with higher density at N-terminus.

evidence.quote_or_span: To evaluate the sensitivity of our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system as compared to more classical, lower-throughput microtiter plate methods, 480 clones were isolated from library A and placed in 96-well plates.
The sequencing of the mutated gene in the six clones reveals that they contain from 1 to 6 point mutations (average of 2.5) at the amino acid level
Mutations are located all along the protein, with a higher density at the N-terminus

evidence.quote_or_span: The lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems has proven to be a major bottleneck when it comes to creating biocatalysts via directed evolution and protein engineering approaches.
most of the enzymes screened to date are expressed within the cytoplasm of Escherichia coli cells, which means that a lysis step must take place inside the droplets for enzyme activity to be assayed.
we take advantage of the excellent secretion abilities of the yeast Yarrowia lipolytica to describe a highly efficient expression system particularly suitable for the droplet-based microfluidic HTS.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis to use Y. lipolytica secretion for HTS.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to express and secrete target enzymes in Y. lipolytica.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Codon-optimized A. niger genes integrated at zeta docking platform will yield high secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design expression cassettes for five A. niger hydrolytic genes (xlnb, xlnc, cbha, egla, pep1) in Y. lipolytica JMY2566 with zeta docking platform.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Five genes were codon-optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566 with a zeta docking platform for consistent integration.

evidence.quote_or_span: The five genes were codon optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566
This strain possesses a zeta docking platform that favors integration at that specific locus

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the enzymes be overproduced and secreted?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Enzymes will be secreted as major proteins in supernatant.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Grow transformants and analyze crude supernatant by SDS-PAGE.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All five enzymes were successfully overproduced and secreted as major proteins in crude supernatant without extensive optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each of the target enzymes was successfully overproduced and secreted as a major protein in the crude supernatant.
All the enzymes demonstrated high expression levels without requiring any extensive optimization of growth conditions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the secreted enzymes active?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secreted enzymes retain activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assay activity with fluorogenic substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion. Endoglucanase A (egla) displayed low activity on cellobiose substrate, likely due to substrate specificity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to droplet encapsulation despite egla limitation.

evidence.quote_or_span: All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion.
JMY4343 (egla) displayed a low activity with this substrate, seemingly due to the endocellulase activity of endoglucanase A

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can Y. lipolytica cells be encapsulated and grown in droplets?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Optimized encapsulation conditions will allow single-cell growth and secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate single Y. lipolytica cells in 20 pl droplets with continuous stirring; produce droplets at 2500–3000/s.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Droplets produced at 2500–3000/s; cells grew and secreted enzymes after 16 h incubation.

evidence.quote_or_span: cell suspensions were also continuously stirred during the encapsulation process to prevent cell sedimentation
Droplets were produced at a rate of 2500 to 3000 per second via hydrodynamic flow focusing
Droplets were collected off chip in a glass vial and incubated for 16 h at 28 °C to allow cell growth and enzyme secretion.
the droplets contained dozens of cells after incubation, showing that Y. lipolytica was able to replicate in the droplets.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can xylanase activity be detected with low variability?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Xylanase activity will show low CV due to uniform expression and controlled incubation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Picoinject xylobiose-based coumarin substrate, incubate on-chip, and measure fluorescence; use sulforhodamine B for optical encoding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Xylanase activity of two strains (xlnb and xlnc) was detected with low variability (CV 3%). Positive droplet percentage for xlnb was 2.7%.

evidence.quote_or_span: Both strains showed remarkably low variability in their signal distributions.
coefficient of variation [CV] of 3%
For JMY4346 (xlnb) strain, a positive droplet population showing xylanase activity (2.7% of the droplets [theoretically 3% if $\lambda = 0.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why is variability low?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low variability is due to Y. lipolytica expression system and delay line design.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attribute low variability to constitutive promoter and uniform incubation time.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low variability attributed to Y. lipolytica expression system and precise control of incubation time via delay line design.

evidence.quote_or_span: This narrow phenotypic distribution at the single cell level within a monoclonal population is highly desirable for enzymatic screening applications and is due to a combination of two factors.
the expression system developed in Y. lipolytica under a strong constitutive promoter is characterized by low variability in expression levels
the delay line structure design, made of constrictions and expansions, ensured that average droplet residence time, and hence incubation time, displayed little variance

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can cellobiohydrolase and protease be detected?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cellobiohydrolase and protease will show activity with comparable narrow signal distribution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assay cellobiohydrolase (cbha) and protease (pep1) with respective fluorogenic substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cellobiohydrolase and protease activities were detected with comparable narrow signal distribution, demonstrating efficient and reliable detection.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each strain showed hydrolytic activity against the respective substrate with comparable narrow signal distribution, while no activity was detected for the other strains.
These results demonstrate that our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system is an efficient and reliable method for detecting endoxylanase, cellobiohydrolase, and protease activity.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create mutant libraries for thermostability screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc will yield variants with altered thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc gene with low (library A, ~2 mutations/kb) and medium (library B, >4 mutations/kb) mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries A and B generated.

evidence.quote_or_span: Libraries were generated from xlnc gene using error-prone PCR.
We built libraries of error-prone xlnc variants using a two-step PCR amplification according to [30].

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can thermostable variants be enriched by heat shock?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Heat shock at 90 °C for 30 s will inactivate less stable variants, enriching thermostable ones.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate libraries, apply heat shock (90 °C, 30 s), and assay xylanase activity before and after.

tool_or_method.name: Integrated microfluidic screening device with heat shock module

evolution_trajectory.observation: Before heat shock, 27% of clones in library A and 9% in library B displayed xylanase activity; none had greater activity than wild-type. After heat shock, 1% (8 clones) in library A and 0.17% (1 clone) in library B had higher activities than wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate hits by microtiter plate and sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the droplet-based microfluidic HTS system described above, both libraries were analyzed before and after a heat shock of 90 °C for 30 s
The JMY4510 (xlnc) wild-type, library A, and library B were encapsulated in 20 pl droplets as described above.
To generate the heat shock, the droplets were run through PTFE tubing (0.56 mm × 1.07 mm) immersed in a 90 °C water bath. The flow rate was set such that residence time within the tubing was 30 s.
The droplets were then collected in a glass vial and reloaded into the integrated screening device to assay post-heat-shock levels of xylanase activity.

After exposure to heat shock, 1% of the clones (8 clones) in library A were found to have higher activities than the wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ]), while this percentage was 0.17% (1 clone) for library B.

Before exposure to heat shock, 27% of the clones (216 clones) in library A and 9% of the clones (54 clones) in library B displayed xylanase activity. None of these clones were found to have greater activity than wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the hits truly thermostable? What mutations do they carry?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Microtiter plate retest will confirm thermostability; sequencing will reveal mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Isolate 480 clones from library A, retest in microtiter plates, and sequence six confirmed thermostable mutants.

tool_or_method.name: Microtiter plate assay and Sanger sequencing

evolution_trajectory.observation: Microtiter plate screening identified six thermostable mutants, similar to droplet-based HTS results. Sequencing revealed 1–6 point mutations (average 2.5) with higher density at N-terminus.

evidence.quote_or_span: To evaluate the sensitivity of our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system as compared to more classical, lower-throughput microtiter plate methods, 480 clones were isolated from library A and placed in 96-well plates.
The sequencing of the mutated gene in the six clones reveals that they contain from 1 to 6 point mutations (average of 2.5) at the amino acid level
Mutations are located all along the protein, with a higher density at the N-terminus

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Platform validated for xylanase thermostability screening.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Platform is limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays; some enzymes show lower signal.
evolution_trajectory.decision: State limitations based on egla low activity and the need for compatible fluorescent substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The platform is limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays; endoglucanase A had low activity on cellobiose substrate.
